title: Deodorant on Polyester
stainChemistry: Deodorant/antiperspirant stains are caused by aluminum compounds (aluminum chlorohydrate) reacting with sweat proteins and fabric dyes. On polyester, the aluminum salt creates a white residue that bonds to fiber. Over time, the aluminum-protein complex yellows and hardens, becoming increasingly resistant to standard washing. The buildup is cumulative — it gets worse with each wear.
whyThisWorks: An acidic treatment (white vinegar or dilute acetic acid) dissolves the aluminum salt deposits on polyester. Surfactant lifts the dissolved residue. For yellowish buildup, an oxidizer addresses the protein-aluminum complex. Prevention is equally important: applying deodorant and letting it dry completely before dressing dramatically reduces fiber transfer.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:5) to dissolve aluminum salt deposits
2. Apply NSD to lift dissolved residue
3. If yellowing remains: apply H2O2 6% on colorfast fabrics
4. Flush with cool water. Apply dilute acetic acid as final rinse
warnings: Always test on hidden area first

homeSolutions.agent: This stain is cumulative — it gets worse every wear
homeSolutions.instruction: Deodorant on polyester isn't a one-time spill; it's aluminum salt plus sweat protein that builds up wash after wash. The yellow/white buildup you see is usually dozens of tiny overlays. That means: (1) one treatment won't always fully clear cumulative buildup, and (2) PREVENTION matters as much as treatment — let deodorant dry fully before putting on polyester, and stop using fabric softener which locks the residue in.

homeSolutions.agent: Lift dry flake residue — brush, don't rub
homeSolutions.instruction: Any dry white flakes on the surface: lift off with a soft brush (an old toothbrush is perfect) or the edge of a dampened sponge. Brush outward from the center, don't rub — rubbing grinds aluminum crystals deeper into the polyester weave where later steps can't reach them.

homeSolutions.agent: The key step: white vinegar dissolves aluminum salts
homeSolutions.instruction: Pour white vinegar DIRECTLY on the stain (or soak the underarm area in 1 cup vinegar + 2 cups cold water if treating a whole garment). Let it dwell 15–20 minutes for fresh stains; up to an hour for heavy buildup. Vinegar's mild acid is THE specific tool that breaks the aluminum-chlorohydrate bond holding the stain to the fiber. No other household product does this job — vinegar is the whole fight.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix vinegar with bleach or hydrogen peroxide in the same dwell — gas. Rinse fully between if you plan to use peroxide next.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn dish soap — lift dissolved residue
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply a small drop of Dawn on the treated area. Work it in with a fingertip or soft toothbrush in SHORT strokes — not back-and-forth rubbing. Let sit 5 minutes. Dawn emulsifies the oil component of the deodorant carrier so the dissolved aluminum rinses away cleanly rather than re-depositing on the fiber.

For cumulative yellow staining (weeks or months of buildup), an enzyme pretreat handles the protein side of the combination stain. Apply OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout per label and dwell 20–30 minutes. Skip this step on fresh stains — it's only needed for set yellow.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold machine wash — NO fabric softener
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on cold with your normal detergent. Do NOT use fabric softener — it's the #1 reason deodorant buildup comes back wash after wash. Add a half-cup of white vinegar to the rinse cycle instead (vinegar acts as a natural softener AND neutralizes any alkaline residue from detergent).

Hang or lay flat to air-dry. Inspect underarms in daylight. If yellow or white buildup remains, repeat steps 3–6. Cumulative stains often need 2–3 full rounds. DO NOT tumble-dry on heat until fully clear — dryer heat fuses residual aluminum salts into polyester permanently.

If buildup survives 2–3 home rounds, or has been heat-set by a dryer over months, take it in. Tell them: "Cumulative antiperspirant buildup on polyester, treated with vinegar soak + Dawn + enzyme. No fabric softener used (going forward). Residual yellow/white remains." Pros use concentrated acetic acid + commercial enzyme + oxidizer in sequence. Going forward: switch to aluminum-free deodorant on polyester garments you want to preserve.

steps: 1. Lift dry flaked residue with a soft brush or the edge of a dampened sponge — do not rub the stain, rubbing drives aluminum salts deeper into the polyester weave.
2. Apply white vinegar directly to the stain to dissolve aluminum deposits — 10-15 min dwell time. Dab with a clean sponge to lift, never rub.
3. Blot with clean white cloth or sponge. Repeat vinegar if needed.
4. Apply Dawn dish soap and work gently with a soft brush in short strokes — 5 min dwell time. Do not rub across the weave.
5. For yellowed cumulative buildup, pretreat with an enzyme laundry detergent (Tide or Persil) before the vinegar step — enzymes break the sweat-protein side of the combination stain.
6. Rinse with cold water. Air dry flat.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional for specialized treatment
warnings: Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first
Air dry until stain is confirmed gone — heat sets most stains
Do NOT use OxiClean or oxidizing agents on protein stains — they can set the stain permanently

neverDo: No strong alkali at high temperature
fiberSensitivities: Oleophilic — grabs and holds oil stains tenaciously
Retains odors due to lipophilic surface
Most chemicals are safe on polyester

escalation.when: If deodorant stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is deodorant on polyester, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
